elements.sent-2411-001: We know from Ram\'irez and Rider \cite{DiffusionAtTheRandomMatrixHardEdge that the hard edge of the spectrum of the $\beta$-Laguerre ensemble converges, in the high-dimensional limit, to the bottom of the spectrum of the stochastic Bessel operator.}
elements.sent-2411-002: Using stochastic analysis techniques, we show that, in the high temperatures limit, the rescaled eigenvalues point process of the stochastic Bessel operator converges to a limiting point process characterized with coupled stochastic differential equations.
elements.sent-2411-003: I wish to thank Laure Dumaz for her precious help throughout this research project.
elements.sent-2411-004: Coulomb gases, also known as $\beta$-ensembles, are probability measures on sets of points.
elements.sent-2411-005: In statistical physics, these points are seen as elementary particles on the real line confined under a random potential while repelling each other.
elements.sent-2411-006: The Dyson parameter $\beta$ acts as an inverse temperature and can affect the shape of the potential and the strength of the repulsive force.
elements.sent-2411-007: When $\beta$ is close to $\infty$, the system becomes ordered due to high repulsion between the particles.
elements.sent-2411-008: Conversely, when $\beta$ is close to $0$, weak repulsion leads to a disordered system.
elements.sent-2411-009: In recent years, $\beta$-ensembles and their connections to stochastic operators have attracted significant interest (see \cite{MatrixModelsForBetaEnsembles , \cite{GlobalSpectrumFluctuations , \cite{GapUniversalityOfGeneralizedWignerAndBetaEnsembles , \cite{BetaEnsemblesStochasticAirySpectrum , \cite{BulkUniversalityOfGeneralBetaEnsemblesWithNonConvexPotential , \cite{UniversalityOfGeneralBetaEnsembles , \cite{OrthogonalAndSymplecticMatrixModels , \cite{LocalSemicircleLawInTheBulkForGaussianBetaEnsemble , \cite{ContinuumLimitsOfRandomMatrices , \cite{LocalSemicircleLawAtTheSpectralEdgeForGaussianBetaEnsembles ).}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The size $n$ $\beta$-Laguerre ensemble is a two-parameter family of distributions on $(\mathbb{R _+^{* )^n$ with density function with respect to the Lebesgue measure \begin{equation}\label{densityLaguerre} P_n^{\beta, a} \big(\gl^{(n)}_0 \leqslant \gl^{(n)}_1 \ldots \leqslant \gl^{(n)}_{n-1}\big)= \frac{1}{Z_{n}^{\beta,a}} \prod_{i < j} |\gl^{(n)}_i - \gl^{(n)}_j|^\beta \times \prod_{k=0}^{n-1} (\gl^{(n)}_k)^{\frac{\beta}{2}(a+1) -1} e^{-\frac{\beta}{2} \gl^{(n)}_k} 1_{\gl^{(n)}_k > 0}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: The parameters satisfy $\beta>0$ and $a > -1$, and $Z_{n ^{\beta,a}$ is an explicitely computable normalizing constant.. When $\beta=1$ (resp.}
elements.sent-2411-012: This connection to random matrices was extended to any $\beta>0$ by Dimitriu and Edelman \cite[Theorem 3.4]{MatrixModelsForBetaEnsembles , adapting earlier work from Silverstein \cite{TheSmallestEigenvalueOfALargeDimensionalWishartMatrix .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: They found a set of bidiagonal random matrices $\big(L_{n ^\beta\big)_{n>0}$ of size $(n,n+a)$ such that $\mathcal{P _n^{\beta, a}$ is the density of the eigenvalues of $L_{n ^\beta(L_{n ^\beta)^T$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: When $n$ tends to infinity, the rescaled empirical measure of the eigenvalues converges weakly a.s.
elements.sent-2411-015: We refer to \cite{OnSpectralMeasuresOfRandomJacobiMatrices for a review of the asymptotic global statistics of classical $\beta$-ensembles.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Because of the positivity constraint for the eigenvalues of $L_{n ^\beta(L_{n ^\beta)^T$, $0$ is called a hard edge for the $\beta$-Laguerre ensemble.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: To study local statistics at the hard edge in the high-dimensional limit, Ram\'irez and Rider \cite{DiffusionAtTheRandomMatrixHardEdge introduced a stochastic operator as limiting object for the $\beta$-Laguerre ensemble.}
elements.sent-2411-018: For $\beta>0$ and $a>-1$, the stochastic Bessel operator (known as SBO) is the random differential operator \begin{equation}G^{\beta,a} = -\frac{1}{m(x)}\frac{d}{d x}\Big(\frac{1}{s(x)}\frac{d}{d x}\Big), m(x)=\exp\big(-(a+1)x-\frac{2}{\sqrt{\beta}}B(x)\big), \quad s(x)=\exp\big(ax+\frac{2}{\sqrt{\beta}}B(x)\big),\end{equation} defined on a subset of $L^2(\mathbb{R _+,m)$ with Dirichlet and Neumann boundary conditions at $0$ and at infinity, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-019: They showed a connection between the eigenvalues of the $\beta$-Laguerre ensemble at the hard edge and the low-lying eigenvalues of the SBO:.
elements.sent-2411-020: Moreover, with $0<\gl^{(n) _0 < \gl^{(n) _1 < \ldots < \gl^{(n) _n$ the ordered points of the $\beta$-Laguerre ensemble of size $n$, \begin{equation}\big\lbrace n\gl^{(n)}_0, n\gl^{(n)}_1, \ldots, n\gl^{(n)}_k\big\rbrace \Longrightarrow \big\lbrace \Lambda^{\beta,a}(0)<\Lambda^{\beta,a}(1)<\ldots <\Lambda^{\beta,a}(k)\big\rbrace,\end{equation} (jointly in law) for any fixed $k<\infty$ as $n \uparrow \infty$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper, we study the convergence of the lowest eigenvalues of the SBO in the high temperatures limit, when the inverse temperature parameter $\beta$ tends to $0$.
elements.sent-2411-022: When $\beta$ tends to $0$, the smallest eigenvalues of the SBO get close to the hard edge at $0$ at an exponential rate.
elements.sent-2411-023: In order to get a non trivial limit, we therefore consider the rescaled eigenvalues \begin{equation}\label{eq_eig_resc} \mu^{\beta}(k) := \beta \ln\big(1/\Lambda^{\beta,a}(k)\big), \ k \geqslant 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-024: Note that we thus reverse the ordering of the eigenvalues, and that any $\mu^{\beta (k)$ is positive for $\beta$ small enough.}
elements.sent-2411-025: We restrict ourselves to the case $a>0$ because the estimations computed in Section \ref{sec_6_expl and Section \ref{sec_6_tight differ if $a \in (-1,0]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Since the parameter $a$ is set, we omit it from our notations going forward.
elements.sent-2411-027: The convergence holds for a well chosen topology of measures on $\mathbb{R _+$, corresponding to a left-vague/right-weak topology (see after Proposition \ref{prop_cv_eig for more details).}}
elements.sent-2411-028: The limiting point process is simple in the sense that all its points are distinct almost surely.
elements.sent-2411-029: We will characterize it (similarly to the SBO eigenvalues) through the coupled diffusions (\ref{SDEq+ ) and (\ref{SDEq- ).}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Usually, one expects that, when the temperature is high, the limiting point process is no longer repulsive and corresponds to a Poisson point process as the noise becomes dominant.
elements.sent-2411-031: Here, we get a different result.
elements.sent-2411-032: It comes from the competition between the strong repulsive interaction and attraction at the hard edge for small $\beta$ (see \eqref{densityLaguerre ).}
elements.sent-2411-033: Because of this interaction, the repulsive factor does not disappear in the limit.

elements.sent-2411-034: If $\psi$ solves the eigenvalues equation $\mathfrak{G ^{\beta,a \psi=\Lambda^{\beta,a \psi$ with initial conditions $\psi(0)=c_{0}$, $\psi^{\prime (0)=c_{1}$, then Dumaz, Li and Valk\'o \cite[Proposition 7]{OperatorLevelHardToSoft showed that $\left(\psi, \psi^{\prime}\right)$ is the unique strong solution of the stochastic differential equation system: \begin{equation}\mathrm{d} \psi(x)=\psi^{\prime}(x) \mathrm{d} x, \quad \mathrm{d} \psi^{\prime}(x)=\frac{2}{\sqrt{\beta}} \psi^{\prime}(x) \mathrm{d} B(x)+\left((a+\frac{2}{\beta}) \psi^{\prime}(x)-\Lambda^{\beta,a} e^{-x} \psi(x)\right) \mathrm{d} x,\end{equation} with the corresponding initial conditions, and $B$ the Brownian motion from Definition \ref{def_3_SBO .}}}}}}

elements.subsection-2411-003: Description of the limiting point process
elements.sent-2411-068: Let us now define $r_\mu$, the limiting diffusion of $q^{\beta _\mu$, which will characterize the limiting point process from Theorem \ref{thm_main .}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Its definition involves Brownian motions with drift reflected downwards at $0$.
elements.sent-2411-070: By definition, a Brownian motion with drift $\alpha$ reflected downwards at $0$ is a diffusion Markov process with infinitesimal operator $G \; :\; f \in \cD \mapsto \frac{1 {2 f'' + \alpha f'$ acting on the domain \begin{equation}\cD :=\Big\lbrace f \in C_b[0,\infty[, Gf \in C_b\big([0,\infty[\big),\; \lim_{x \downarrow 0} f'(x) = 0\Big\rbrace\,,\end{equation} where $C_b\big([0,\infty[\big)$ denotes the continuous and bounded functions on $[0,\infty[$.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Using the Skorohod problem, we can write this diffusion as \begin{equation}B(t) +\alpha t - \sup_{s \leqslant t} \big(B(s) +\alpha s\big)\vee 0\,,\end{equation} where $B$ is a Brownian motion starting at $0$ or any negative point.
elements.sent-2411-072: To define the limiting diffusion $r_\mu$, we use the same Brownian motion $W$ as in the diffusions $q_\mu^+$ and $q_\mu^-$ from (\ref{SDEq+ ) and (\ref{SDEq- ).}}
elements.sent-2411-074: It then follows a Brownian motion with drift $a/4$ reflected downwards at $0$ (built from $W$) \begin{equation}\label{eq_r+} r^+:= W(t) + at/4 - \sup_{s \leqslant t} \big(W(s) +as/4\big)\vee 0\,\end{equation} until its first hitting time of the critical line $c_\mu(t)$ from \eqref{criticalline.}
elements.sent-2411-075: If it reaches $t \mapsto c_\mu(t)$ in a finite time, it immediately restarts at $0$ at this time, then follows a reflected downwards at $0$ Brownian motion with another drift $-(a+1)/4$ (also built from $W$) \begin{align}\label{eq_r-} r^-:= W(t) - (a+1)t/4 - \sup_{s \leqslant t} \big(W(s) - (a+1)s/4\big)\vee 0 end{align} until its first hitting time of the critical line $c_\mu(t)$ from \eqref{criticalline.}
elements.sent-2411-076: And so on, $r_\mu$ alternating between $r^+$ and $r^-$ each time it hits $t \mapsto c_\mu(t)$ and restarts at $0$.
elements.sent-2411-077: Note that the probability that $r^+$ reaches the critical line decreases with time.
elements.sent-2411-078: On the other hand, since $a>0$, $r^-$ almost surely hits the critical line in a finite time.

elements.sent-2411-108: We decompose the trajectory of $q^+$ into three parts.
elements.sent-2411-109: First, it reaches the axis $x= 0$ in a short time (descent from $\infty$).
elements.sent-2411-110: Then, it spends a time of order $O(1)$ in the region $[c(t)+\delta,0]$ and behaves like $r^+$, the reflected Brownian motion with drift $a/4$ from \eqref{eq_r+ .}
elements.sent-2411-111: Finally, if it approaches the critical line $t \mapsto c(t)$ closer than $\delta$, then it explodes with high probability within a short time (explosion to $-\infty$).
elements.sent-2411-112: Recall the first hitting times $\xi_0^+(0)<\xi_0^-(0)$ of the critical line $c$ by the diffusion $r_\mu$ from Definition \ref{def_0_limdif .}

elements.sent-2411-117: The rest of this section is dedicated to the proof of Proposition \ref{prop_an .}
elements.sent-2411-118: Recall that the diffusion $q^-$ differs from its counterpart $q^+$ only by its constant drift component $-(a+1)/4$ (instead of $+a/4$ for $q^+$), which makes $q^-$ decrease faster than $q^+$.
elements.sent-2411-119: We prove the results for the diffusion $q^+$, they extend to the diffusion $q^-$ with the same arguments.
elements.sent-2411-120: We introduce the stationary diffusion $\overline{q}$ on $\mathbb{R _+$, which we use to approximate $q^+$ in the region where the drift component $\exp\big[-\frac{1 {\beta}\big(q^+(t)-c(t)\big)\big]$ becomes negligible as $\beta$ tends to $0$: \begin{equation}\label{eq_an_stat} \mathrm{d} \overline{q}(t)= \mathrm{d} W(t) +\frac{1}{4}\big(a-e^{\overline{q}(t)/\beta}\big)\mathrm{d} t.\\\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-121: It suffices to prove property $(a)$ for the diffusion $\overline{q}$, which bounds the diffusion $q^+$ from above.
elements.sent-2411-123: As $\beta$ tends to $0$, when the diffusion $\overline{q}$ is above the level $l_1$, the term of leading order in the right-hand side of (\ref{eq_an_stat ) is $e^{\overline{q (t)/\beta}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-124: Consider the ordinary differential equation on $\mathbb{R _+$: \begin{equation}\label{eq_an_descent_det} \mathrm{d} y(t) := \frac{1}{4}(a-e^{y(t)/(2\beta)})\mathrm{d} t, \ y(0)=\infty,\end{equation} which has for solution $y(t) = -2\beta \ln\big(\frac{1 {a (1-e^{-at/(8\beta) )\big)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: The time $t_1$ at which $y$ reaches the level $l_1/2$ has the asymptotics \begin{equation}\label{eq_an_descent_det_time} t_1 = 8\beta e^{-l_1/(4\beta)} + o(\beta e^{-l_1/(4\beta)}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-130: On the event $\mathcal{E '_1$ and while $\overline{q _1(t) \geqslant l_1/2$, we have \begin{equation}\left| \frac{W(t)}{\overline{q}_1(t)} \right| \leqslant \frac{\beta^2}{l_1/2}\,, \ \text{ i.e } \ \left| \frac{W(t)}{\overline{q}_1(t)} \right| \leqslant 2\beta^{5/4}\end{equation} and thus $\overline{q _1(t) + W(t) \geqslant \overline{q _1(t)/2$ for $\beta$ small enough.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: Therefore the diffusion $\overline{q _1$ is bounded from above by $y$ for $\beta$ small enough and hits the level $l_1/2$ before time $t_1$.}
elements.sent-2411-132: Since $\big\lvert \overline{q _1(t)-\overline{q (t) \big\rvert \leqslant \beta^2$ before time $t_1$, for $\beta$ small enough, the diffusion $\overline{q}$ hits the level $l_1$ before time $t_1$ .}}
elements.sent-2411-133: After the level $l_1$ is reached, we use the Brownian motion $W(t_1+\cdot)-W(t_1)$ to reach $x=0$ in a short additional time.

elements.sent-2411-142: To that end, we bound the diffusion $\overline{q _0(t)$ from below and above by two reflected diffusions $r^+_1$ and $r^+_2$ that converge to $r^+$ as $\beta$ tends to $0$.}
elements.sent-2411-143: We set the level $l_2:=\beta^{1/6}$, so that $l_2=o(\delta) \text{ and \delta =o(\sqrt{l_2 )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-145: Let $r_1^+$ be the following diffusion, reflected downwards at the barrier $-l_2$: \begin{equation}r_1^+(t) := -l_2 + W(t)+at/4 -e^{-\frac{l_2}{\beta}}T/4 - \sup_{s \leqslant t} \big(W(s)+as/4 -e^{-\frac{l_2}{\beta}}T/4\big)\vee 0.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-150: To prove that this upper bound holds with high probability as $\beta$ tends to $0$, we use the following result, that shows how unlikely it becomes for the diffusion $\overline{q _0(t)$ to hit the level $l_2$ before any negative level.}

elements.sent-2411-157: To prove property $(c)$, we choose $\eta:=2l_2$ and show that there exists an event of probability going to $1$ as $\beta$ tends to $0$ on which, for $\beta$ small enough, $\tau_\delta<\eta/2$ and $\tau_{-\infty <\eta/2$.}
elements.sent-2411-159: We use the variations of the Brownian motion $W$ to cross the critical line $c(t)$.
elements.sent-2411-160: The upper bound $q_{\delta ^+(t)\leqslant -\mu +\delta+W(t)+\frac{a {4 t$ implies that $\tau_\delta < l_2$ on the event \begin{equation}\mathcal{E}_3 := \bigg\lbrace \underset{[0,l_2]}\inf\Big\lbrace W(s)+\frac{a}{4}s + \frac{1}{4}s +2\delta \Big\rbrace<0 \bigg\rbrace,\end{equation} and the Brownian tail bound from (\ref{eq_Browniantail2 ) shows that $\mathbb{P}\big(\mathcal{E _3\big) \rightarrow 1$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-161: Note that the inclusion of events $\mathcal{E '_3 \subset \Big\lbrace \inf\big\lbrace t \geqslant 0, \ r^+(t)+t/4\leqslant-2\delta \big\rbrace< l_2\Big\rbrace$ ensures that, on a subevent of $\mathcal{E _2$ (where $\lvert q^+(\tau_c)-r^+(\tau_c)\rvert<\delta$ if $\tau_c<\infty$) of probability going to $\mathbb{P (\mathcal{E _2)$ as $\beta$ tends to $0$, the diffusion $r^+$ hits the critical line $c(t)$ while the diffusion $q^+$ crosses this line, between times $\tau_c$ and $\tau_c+\eta$.}}}}

elements.section-2411-005: Tightness of the explosion times measures
elements.sent-2411-167: Recall the measure of the explosion times $\nu_\mu^\beta$ from \eqref{explosionsq- .}
elements.sent-2411-168: We prove in this section that there are $\beta_0>0$ and $\alpha>0$, such that, for all $\epsilon>0$, there exist a finite time $T_\epsilon$ and a finite number of explosions $N_\epsilon$ so that: \begin{equation}\label{eq_tight_main0} \underset{\beta \leqslant \beta_0}\inf \mathbb{P}\bigg(\Big\lbrace\nu_\mu^\beta\big([0,\alpha T_\epsilon]\big)\leqslant N_\epsilon \Big\rbrace \bigcap \Big\lbrace \nu_\mu^\beta\big([\alpha T_\epsilon,\infty[\big)=0\Big\rbrace\bigg)>1-\epsilon.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-169: Introduce $\mathcal{L_\mu^\beta}$, the law of the random measure $\nu_\mu^\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-170: The bound (\ref{eq_tight_main0 ) gives us the tightness condition: \begin{equation}\label{eq_tight_compact} \exists \beta_0, \ \forall \epsilon>0, \ \exists K_\epsilon \text{ compact}, \ \underset{\beta<\beta_0}\sup \ \mathcal{L_\mu^\beta}(K_\epsilon)\geqslant 1-\epsilon.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-171: Indeed, the set $\mathring{K _\epsilon :=\big\lbrace \mu \in \mathcal{P , \ \mu([0,\alpha T_\epsilon]) \leqslant N_\epsilon \text{ and \mu\big([\alpha T_\epsilon,\infty[\big)=0\big\rbrace$, where $\mathcal{P}$ is the space of locally finite measures on $\mathbb{R _+$, satisfies the following conditions: \begin{equation}\label{eq_tightcompact2} \begin{split} \underset{\mu \in \mathring{K}_\epsilon}\sup \mu(\mathbb{R_+})<\infty, \underset{t>0}\inf \ \underset{\mu \in \mathring{K}_\epsilon}\sup \mu\big([t,\infty[\big)=0, \end{split}\end{equation} where the second condition holds since, for any measure $\mu$ in $\mathring{K _\epsilon$ and $t\geqslant \alpha T_\epsilon, \ \mu \big([t,\infty[\big)=0$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-172: It thus fulfills the Kallenberg criterion for weak relative compactness (see \cite{RandomMeasures ), and its compact closure $K_\epsilon$ verifies the tightness condition (\ref{eq_tight_compact ).}}
elements.sent-2411-173: Prokhorov's theorem gives us the relative compactness of the family $(\mathcal{L_\mu^\beta )_{\beta<\beta_0}$ in $\mathcal{M ^1(\mathcal{P )$, the set of probability measures on $\mathcal{P}$, and thus concludes the proof of Proposition \ref{prop_cv_expl .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-174: The rest of this section is dedicated to the proof of (\ref{eq_tight_main0 ).}
elements.sent-2411-175: We first show a preliminary result that will be helpful to control the number of explosions.
elements.sent-2411-176: Recall that $\xi^+=\xi_\beta^+(0)$ is the first explosion time of the diffusion $q^+$ from \eqref{SDEq+ , started from $\infty$ at time $0$.}
elements.sent-2411-177: We fix a deterministic $\delta_0$ such that $0<\delta_0<\mu/4$.
elements.sent-2411-178: Recall the definition of the critical line $c_\mu$ in \eqref{criticalline .}
elements.sent-2411-179: When the diffusion $q^+$ is in the region between $-c_\mu(t)+\delta_0$ and $-\delta_0$, we have the lower bound, for $\beta$ small enough: \begin{equation}\label{eq_ctrl_bound} a-e^{q^+(t)/\beta}-e^{-\frac{1}{\beta}(\mu(t)+q^+(t))} \geqslant a-2e^{-\delta_0/\beta}\geqslant 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-180: Introduce the diffusion $\widehat{q}$ on $\mathbb{R _+$, defined as the Brownian motion $W(t)$ started from $-\delta_0$ at time $0$ and reflected downwards at $-\delta_0$: $\widehat{q (t) := -\delta_0+W(t)-\sup_{s \leqslant t \big(W(s)\big)\vee 0.$ The bound (\ref{eq_ctrl_bound ) shows that, for $\beta$ small enough, the diffusion $q^+$ is bounded from below by the diffusion $\widehat{q}$ on each Brownian trajectory, up until the first hitting time of $c_\mu(t)+\delta_0$ by $q^+$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-181: Set $t>0$ and introduce the event $\mathcal{E := \big\lbrace\underset{s \in [0, t]}\sup\big\lvert W(s) \big\rvert <\mu/4\big\rbrace$.}
elements.sent-2411-182: By the Brownian tail bound (\ref{eq_Browniantail ), we have $\mathbb{P (\mathcal{E )\geqslant 1 -4e^{-\mu^2/(32t)}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-183: Besides, on the event $\mathcal{E}$, using (\ref{eq_drawdown ), \begin{equation}\forall s \in [0, t], \ \widehat{q}(s) > -\delta_0-\mu/2.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-184: This means that the diffusion $\widehat{q}$ stays above $-\mu$ until time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-185: Thus, for $\beta$ small enough so that (\ref{eq_ctrl_bound ) holds, on the event $\mathcal{E}$, we have $\xi^+>t$.}
elements.sent-2411-186: We now turn to the proof of (\ref{eq_tight_main0 ).. Fix $\epsilon >0$.}
elements.sent-2411-187: We control the diffusion $q_\mu^\beta$ with two diffusions.
elements.sent-2411-188: The first diffusion $Q_1$ starts at time $T_\epsilon$ at position $-1$ and is reflected below the horizontal line $-1$ with drift $a/8$.
elements.sent-2411-189: The second diffusion $Q_2$ starts at time $2T$ at position $c_\mu(T_\epsilon)$, has a drift $a/8$ as well and is also reflected below $-1$.
elements.sent-2411-190: We can choose $T_\epsilon$ high enough such that the diffusions $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ do not hit $c_\mu(t)+1$ with probability greater than $1-\epsilon/10$.
elements.sent-2411-191: Indeed, the sublinearity of the Brownian motion $W(T_\epsilon+t)-W(T_\epsilon)$ is such that \begin{equation}\label{eq_Teps} \exists \ T_0, \forall t \geqslant T_0, \lvert W(T_\epsilon+t)-W(T_\epsilon) \rvert <t/16 \text{ with probability greater than } 1-\epsilon/40.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-048: \begin{equation}\label{eq_Teps} \exists \ T_0, \forall t \geqslant T_0, \lvert W(T_\epsilon+t)-W(T_\epsilon) \rvert <t/16  with probability greater than  1-\epsilon/40.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-192: On the event where (\ref{eq_Teps ) holds, the diffusion $Q_1$ stays above $-1-t/8$ after time $T_0$, and thus above the critical line $c_\mu(t)$.}
elements.sent-2411-193: We now choose $T_\epsilon$ high enough so that $\lvert W(T_\epsilon+t)-W(t) \rvert <c_\mu(T)/4$ with probability greater than $1-\epsilon/40$ until time $T_0$, thus $Q_1$ stays above $c_\mu(t)$ before time $T_0$ as well.
elements.sent-2411-194: Therefore, on an event of probability greater than $1-\epsilon/20$, the diffusion $Q_1$ never hits $c_\mu(t)$.
elements.sent-2411-195: Similar arguments can be used for the second diffusion $Q_2$.
elements.sent-2411-196: The term $-(a+1)/4$ in the drift of the diffusion $q^-$ implies the existence of $\alpha>0$ such that, almost surely, when started before time $2T_\epsilon+1$, the diffusion $q^-$ explodes before time $\alpha T_\epsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-197: If, at time $T_\epsilon$, the diffusion $q_\mu^\beta$ evolves as the diffusion $q^-$, then it almost surely explodes before time $\alpha T_\epsilon$, after which it evolves as $q^+$ and stays above $Q_1$ (for $\beta$ small enough such that $2e^{-1/\beta <a/8$) and does not explode anymore.}
elements.sent-2411-198: After that, $q_\mu^\beta$ behaves as $q^-$ and almost surely explodes one last time before time $\alpha T_\epsilon$, as previously.
elements.sent-2411-199: In this event, $q_\mu^\beta$ stays above the diffusion $Q_2$ after time $2T_\epsilon$ and thus does not explode.
elements.sent-2411-200: Gathering the different cases, we thus obtain the existence of an event of probability greater than $1-\epsilon/2$ on which $q_\mu^\beta$ explodes at most once after time $T_\epsilon$ and does not explode after time $\alpha T_\epsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-201: To conclude the proof of the tightness criterion (\ref{eq_tight_main0 ), we apply Lemma \ref{lem_ctrl to get the existence of a finite $N_\epsilon$ and of an event of probability greater than $1-\epsilon/2$ on which the diffusion $q_\mu^\beta$ explodes at most $N_\epsilon-1$ times before time $T_\epsilon$.}}
elements.sent-2411-202: We proved that, in the high temperatures limit $\beta \rightarrow 0$, the properly rescaled point process of the low-lying eigenvalues of the stochastic Bessel operator converges towards a simple point process on $\mathbb{R _+$, described using coupled SDEs.}
elements.sent-2411-203: This limiting point process keeps a repulsive factor and therefore differs from the Poisson point process found by Dumaz and Labbé \cite{TheStochasticAiryOperatorAtLargeTemperature as the high temperatures limit of the Stochastic Airy operator.}
elements.sent-2411-204: Our result opens research perspectives to understand the properties of this new point process.
elements.sent-2411-206: In this proof we denote by $q$ the diffusion $\overline{q _0^+$.}
elements.sent-2411-207: Set $\gamma<0$, $\tau := \inf\big\lbrace t \geqslant 0, \ q_0^+(t)=l_2 \big\rbrace$ and $\tau':=\inf\big\lbrace t \geqslant 0, \ q_0^+(t)=\gamma \big\rbrace$.. Let $f_\beta(x):=\frac{1 {4}\big(a-\exp(x/\beta)\big)$.}
elements.sent-2411-208: To compute the hitting times of $q$, introduce the scale functions $\mathfrak{s _\beta$ and $\mathfrak{s}$: \begin{equation}s_\beta(x) := \int_{-1}^x\exp\Big(-2\int_0^u f_\beta(v)d v\Big)d u, s(x) := \int_{-1}^x\exp\Big(-2\int_0^u \frac{a}{4}d v\Big)d u=\frac{2}{a}\big(e^{a/2}-e^{ax/2 }\big).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-049: \begin{equation}s_\beta(x) := \int_{-1}^x\exp\Big(-2\int_0^u f_\beta(v)d v\Big)d u, s(x) := \int_{-1}^x\exp\Big(-2\int_0^u \frac{a}{4}d v\Big)d u=\frac{2}{a}\big(e^{a/2}-e^{ax/2 }\big).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-209: The following lemma explicits the asymptotic behavior of $\mathfrak{s _\beta$.}
elements.sent-2411-211: Since $\mathfrak{s _\beta\big(q(\cdot)\big)$ is a local martingale, $\mathfrak{s _\beta\big(q(\cdot\wedge \tau(q) \wedge \tau'(q)\big)$ is a martingale.}}
elements.sent-2411-212: By the stopping theorem, we get: \begin{equation}\mathbb{P}\big(\tau<\tau'\big) = \frac{\mathfrak{s}_\beta(\gamma)-\mathfrak{s}_\beta\big(0\big)}{\mathfrak{s}_\beta(\gamma)-\mathfrak{s}_\beta\big(l_2\big)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-213: Lemma \ref{lem_an_tube_scale readily implies that this probability tends to $0$ as $\beta$ tends to $0$.}
elements.sent-2411-214: Besides, the functions $\ln \mathfrak{s '_\beta$ and $\ln \mathfrak{s '$ are bounded on $[x_0, 0]$, and $x\mapsto e^x$ is uniformly continuous on $[x_0, 0]$ so $\mathfrak{s '_\beta$ converges uniformly to $\mathfrak{s '$ on $[x_0,0]$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-217: We can compute explicitly: \begin{equation}\label{eq_an_tube_scale} \ln \mathfrak{s}_\beta^{'} (x) = -2\int_0^x f_\beta(v)\mathrm{d} v = -2ax+2\int_0^x e^{v/\beta}\mathrm{d} v = -2ax + 2\beta\big(e^{x/\beta}-1\big).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-218: A study of the variations of $\ln \mathfrak{s '_\beta$ shows that $\mathfrak{s '_\beta$ decreases before $\beta\ln(2a)$ and increases afterwards.}}
elements.sent-2411-219: For $\beta$ small enough, $\ln \mathfrak{s '_\beta(l_1)\geqslant \beta e^{l_1/\beta}$, which tends to $\infty$ as $\beta$ tends to $0$, and $\mathfrak{s '_\beta$ increases on $\big[l_1,l_2\big]$, so that: \begin{equation}\mathfrak{s}_\beta\big(l_2\big)\geqslant \int_{l_1}^{l_2}\mathfrak{s}'_\beta(v)\mathrm{d} v \geqslant \big(l_2-l_1\big)\mathfrak{s}'_\beta(l_1),\end{equation} therefore $\mathfrak{s _\beta\big(l_2\big)\longrightarrow \infty$.}}}
